issue: Recent wrong answers were not guaranteed to be shown newest to oldest by event time.
root_cause: The data layer built the wrong-answer list from an ascending-sorted attempt array and used slice(-5), which depends on upstream ordering and does not preserve explicit newest-first semantics. The payload also lacked a stable display timestamp.
effective_fix: Normalize attempt timestamps from all supported fields, sort the full incorrect-event set descending by timestamp, then take the first five records. Preserve ts and answeredAt in the report payload and defensively sort again in the UI before rendering.
failed_approach: UI-only sorting without fixing the report-data builder leaves cloud payloads and any other consumers with inconsistent ordering semantics.
risk_note: Week-over-week comparison logic may still mix display name and telemetry identity. That is a separate issue and should not be silently bundled into recent-wrong fixes.
verification_rule: For any recent-event feature, verify source event timestamps are preserved, full event sets are sorted before truncation, and rendered order is checked against explicit timestamps.
next_time_instruction: If touching parent-report or answer-history views, document data source, event grain, sorting key, dedupe key, and display order before patching.

issue: Long-running quality work regresses when project rules and lessons only exist in chat context.
root_cause: Repository-level engineering memory was missing outside subsystem-specific folders, so new iterations had no guaranteed root-level read/write loop for lessons, change history, and latest status.
effective_fix: Add a root AGENTS.md plus root logs/change_history.jsonl, logs/lessons_learned.jsonl, and reports/latest_iteration_report.md so each iteration has a mandatory pre-read and writeback target.
failed_approach: Relying on model memory or one-off prompts creates repeated rediscovery and repeated bug patterns.
risk_note: These files only help if future iterations read them first and keep them current.
verification_rule: Before meaningful edits, confirm the agent read AGENTS.md, change_history, lessons_learned, and latest_iteration_report; after edits, confirm all three writeback files were updated.
next_time_instruction: When a fix uncovers a reusable rule, convert it into a JSONL lesson with a concrete verification rule and a direct next-time instruction.

issue: After a wrong answer, pressing Enter could acknowledge the wrong-answer gate instead of re-checking the student's corrected answer.
root_cause: The wrong-answer state reused Enter as an acknowledge shortcut whenever focus was no longer in the answer input. After a submit interaction, focus could remain on the submit button, so the next Enter took the acknowledge branch instead of calling check().
effective_fix: After every wrong attempt, return focus to the answer input and treat Enter on the submit button the same as Enter in the answer field, so corrected answers are re-evaluated before any acknowledge shortcut runs.
failed_approach: Assuming the answer input always keeps focus after submit is unsafe in browser UI flows because buttons can retain focus and change keyboard behavior.
risk_note: This fixes the main Enter-path regression but does not change the product decision that moving to the next question after a wrong attempt still requires acknowledge or skip.
verification_rule: For any retryable form flow, test Enter with focus on the input and on the submit button after an error state. Confirm both paths re-run validation instead of triggering unrelated shortcuts.
next_time_instruction: When a wrong-answer gate and a submit action share Enter, verify focus transitions explicitly and prefer submit semantics over navigation semantics.

issue: Parent-report page had ~500 lines of inline domain logic duplicated from concept engines, making testing and maintenance difficult.
root_cause: All domain logic (weakness ranking, recommendation generation, copy text building, practice generation, report enrichment) was inline inside the HTML page IIFE, unreachable by automated tests.
effective_fix: Extract each domain into a standalone IIFE module on window.*, add script tags to load them before the page IIFE, replace inline functions with thin delegates that call through to shared engines with fallbacks, and add enrichReportData at both showDashboard and refreshReport entry points to pre-compute derived fields.
failed_approach: Attempting to replace 300+ lines inline with replace_string_in_file in one shot causes partial matches and orphaned code. Better to use PowerShell array slicing for large block removals.
risk_note: When using enrichReportData, weeklyFocus and recommendations are placed on report.d (not report root) because the page uses r = d.d as the main report object. Tests must check result.d.weeklyFocus, not result.weeklyFocus.
verification_rule: After extracting inline logic to shared engines, always: (1) add fallback in delegate functions, (2) verify enrichReportData is called in ALL data entry paths (not just the initial load), (3) run both spec tests and integration tests.
next_time_instruction: For large inline code extraction: first add script tags, then replace individual functions one at a time with delegates, then use PowerShell for bulk removal of orphaned code blocks. Never attempt to replace 300+ lines in one replace_string_in_file call.

lesson: Weakness cards should have clickable practice links, not just text descriptions. Use getTopicLink() from AIMathRecommendationEngine to resolve topics to module URLs. When DRYing duplicated logic (like moduleMap in renderRemedial vs TOPIC_LINK_MAP in recommendation_engine), prefer delegating to the shared engine. Always add analytics tracking for new clickable actions.
anti_pattern: Rendering next-action as plain text instead of a clickable link makes it impossible for parents to act on recommendations directly. Duplicating moduleMap across inline code and shared engines leads to drift when new modules are added.
effective_pattern: Delegate topic-to-URL resolution to getTopicLink() with fallback to star-pack. Add data-* attributes for analytics tracking on every action link.

lesson: The WoW comparison logic (prev week vs current week) depends on AIMathAttemptTelemetry which uses d.name as userId for localStorage lookup. When extracting to shared module, keep getPrevWeekAttempts as a separate function that wraps the telemetry call, making identity mapping testable. Always provide fallback inline logic in case the shared module fails to load. The WoW module only needs 3 exports: computeWoW (pure data transform), formatDelta (HTML formatting), getPrevWeekAttempts (telemetry wrapper).
anti_pattern: Computing prev-week stats inline inside renderDashboard makes it impossible to unit-test the comparison logic without a DOM. Also, the original inline code computed prevAvgMs but never displayed it — dead code eliminated during extraction.
effective_pattern: Split WoW into pure computation (computeWoW) and telemetry access (getPrevWeekAttempts). The page wires them together. Tests can test computeWoW without mocking localStorage.

lesson: After extracting 10 shared engines from parent-report (iterations 5-11), all remaining inline code is view-layer: DOM manipulation, event handlers, HTML template rendering. Domain logic extraction is complete. The delegate-with-fallback pattern works well: each extraction adds ~20 lines of fallback but makes the domain logic independently testable. The extraction sequence matters: extract pure data transforms first (weakness, recommendation, report_data_builder), then time-windowed aggregations (wow, progress_trend, practice_summary), then output formatters (parent_copy, parent_advice). Test count grew from 10 → 42 across 11 iterations with zero regressions.
anti_pattern: Extracting interactive UI logic (practice card quiz flow, SVG rendering) into shared modules would create modules that still need DOM mocking, defeating the purpose. Stop extracting when only view-layer remains.
effective_pattern: For each extraction: (1) read inline code, (2) create pure-function IIFE module, (3) add script tag, (4) refactor inline to delegate with fallback, (5) create tests, (6) sync mirror, (7) validate + commit. One module per iteration keeps changes atomic and reviewable.

lesson: Question pages store attempt telemetry under a device UUID (u_abc123...) via AIMathCoachLog.getOrCreateUserId(), but parent-report was passing d.name (display name like '小明') to listAttempts/getPrevWeekAttempts. This caused WoW comparison to always show 'no prior data'. The fix is to resolve the device UUID via getOrCreateUserId() for any localStorage telemetry query, and use d.name only for display and cloud API calls (recordPracticeResult).
anti_pattern: Using a human-readable display name as a storage key when a different system uses a UUID as the key. When two identity systems exist (cloud display name vs device UUID), always verify which key each API expects.
effective_pattern: Add a getDeviceUid() helper that resolves the correct localStorage identity, and use it consistently for all local telemetry queries. Keep d.name for cloud/display contexts only. Add a regression test that proves UUID resolves to stored data while display name does not.

lesson: The esc() helper in parent-report only escaped &, <, > but not quotes. When esc() output is used inside HTML attributes (data-weak-topic, href, etc.), unescaped quotes can break attribute boundaries and enable XSS. Standard HTML escaping must include double-quote (") and single-quote (') to be safe in attribute contexts. Current Chinese topic names don't contain quotes, but this is a latent vulnerability that could be exploited if data ever contains user-controlled strings.
anti_pattern: Escaping only angle brackets and ampersands while placing escaped output inside HTML attributes. The 3-char escape (&, <, >) is only safe for text content nodes, NOT for attribute contexts.
effective_pattern: Always escape all 5 HTML special characters: & < > " '. Add a regression test that verifies the esc() function includes quote escaping by reading the source code. When auditing escaping, check every call site to determine if output goes into text content or attribute values.

lesson: The inline practice answer checker used strict string comparison (normAns), so equivalent fractions like 2/4 were rejected when the stored answer was 1/2. Students who understand the concept get penalized for not simplifying. Fix: add parseFrac() + fractionsEqual() using cross-multiplication. Accept equivalent fractions but show a reminder about simplest form.
anti_pattern: Using string equality for math answers. Any numeric or fractional answer should be compared by value, not by string representation.
effective_pattern: Parse fraction strings (n/d) into {n, d} objects. Use cross-multiplication (a.n * b.d === b.n * a.d) for equivalence. Accept correct-but-unsimplified answers with a gentle simplification reminder. Add test cases for both equivalent and non-equivalent fractions.

lesson: Audited 8 escape/sanitize functions across 8 HTML pages. Found 3 patterns: (1) Full escaping with regex /[&<>"']/g — safe in all contexts. (2) Partial escaping (& < > only) — safe for text nodes, vulnerable in attributes. (3) No escaping (String() only) — safe only for textContent, never innerHTML. The critical finding was exam-sprint escapeHtml() at line 815 which was type (2) but used in attribute context at line 1593 (data-qid). Pages with safe implementations: fraction-word-g5, commercial-pack1-fraction-sprint, interactive-g5-midterm1, interactive-g5-national-bank, report/index.html. No-escape safeStr() functions in exam-sprint, g5-grand-slam, and linear are currently safe because they're used only for textContent.
anti_pattern: Assuming all escape functions across pages are equivalent. Each page may have its own implementation with different coverage. Always audit all call sites when fixing an escape function.
effective_pattern: When auditing escape functions: (1) grep for all escape/sanitize function definitions, (2) check which HTML special chars each escapes, (3) check whether output is used in text or attribute context, (4) fix only those used in attribute context without full escaping. Add source-level regression tests that verify the function implementation includes required escaping.

lesson: Practice in parent-report had two modes: single (再練一題) and quiz-3 (三題小測驗). Only quiz-3 called persistPractice. Single-mode silently dropped results. This broke the commercial flow: student practices → results should appear in summary. Fix: in goNext() for non-quiz mode, reset quizRecorded and call persistPractice per answered question. Also, practice results should write to local AIMathAttemptTelemetry (appendAttempt) so aggregate analytics can see them — not just cloud recordPracticeResult.
anti_pattern: Only persisting results at quiz-end when single-practice mode also generates useful data. Assuming cloud writeback alone is sufficient when local analytics pipelines depend on local telemetry.
effective_pattern: Every practice interaction that yields a score/total should persist via both local telemetry (for analytics) and cloud (for report). Use source='parent-report-practice' to distinguish practice attempts from real quiz attempts. Reset quizRecorded flag per question in single mode to allow per-question persistence.

lesson: When explainWrongDetail and buildPracticeFromWrong only cover a few flagship kinds, the parent-report remediation loop degrades into generic advice for the rest of the existing bank. That weakens the commercial promise even if the links and writeback are correct. Existing bank families should have explicit remediation branches before introducing new content.
anti_pattern: Improving parent-report UI and writeback while leaving most wrong-answer kinds on a generic fallback. Parents see targeted cards, but the downstream explanation and similar-practice step still feels generic.
effective_pattern: Audit real bank kind families, add deterministic explanation/practice branches for uncovered existing kinds, and add regression tests that assert each family no longer falls through to the generic cause text. Keep generated answers integer-safe when possible and avoid hint text that trivially reveals the answer.

lesson: Handwritten kind lists are not enough for remediation coverage once multiple bank formats coexist. In this repo, bank payloads are exposed as window.*_BANK arrays in docs/<module>/bank.js rather than a shared const QUESTIONS wrapper. The safest regression pattern is to execute the real bank.js file in a VM sandbox, read the exported array from window, dedupe by topic+kind, and assert each representative bank family resolves to a non-generic explanation and non-empty fallback practice.
anti_pattern: Parsing bank.js with a hardcoded const QUESTIONS regex or testing only synthetic kind strings misses repo-specific wrappers and lets real module kinds drift away from remediation coverage.
effective_pattern: Load real bank.js payloads through vm.runInContext(), discover the exported bank array from window, and use bank-backed tests for commercial/life modules. Follow that with a future source-level audit so new bank kinds fail automatically when remediation mappings are missing.

lesson: A partial bank-backed audit is still too easy to outgrow. The durable gate is to scan every current docs/*/bank.js module, dedupe by topic+kind, and assert both explainWrongDetail and buildPracticeFromWrong stay non-generic across the whole repo. Some legacy bank files may not execute cleanly in vm because they end with a literal \n token after the assignment, so the loader needs a fallback that extracts the assigned JSON array directly.
anti_pattern: Assuming every bank.js file can be executed as-is in a VM, or limiting the audit to a hand-picked module list. That misses format outliers like interactive-g5-midterm1 and interactive-g5-national-bank and weakens the value of the gate.
effective_pattern: Use a two-step loader: try vm execution first, then fall back to parsing window.* = [...] assignments with a regex that tolerates trailing literal \n. Run this audit as part of the parent-report test suite so new bank kinds break CI immediately instead of silently shipping generic remediation.
